posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Can you drink better Bourgogne rouge than Lafarge and Chevillon?
I do like Bachelet but these two just really hit the sweet spot for Bourgogne.

What is your favorite(s)?

BTW, 2012 Lafarge tonight. Refreshing. Red fruited. Not long but very satisfying.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: I love the Chevillon Bourgogne. I miss being able to buy Mugneret-Gibourg.

posts.name: Pat Burton
posts.cooked: I picked up some Hudelot Noellat but haven’t tasted one yet. Do you have thoughts Don?

posts.name: Levi Pinsky
posts.cooked: 2010 Glantenay was/is pretty spectacular.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Haven’t had the Hudelot Bourgogne.
I love the Bourgogne from the sisters but I think that Lafarge and Chevillon seem to really capture the essence of what Bourgogne should be.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Arnoux and Tremblay are pretty special.
I continue to enjoy Roumier and Barthod 2005s.

posts.name: A Shegedyn
posts.cooked:
So I don’t know if this counts as Bourgogne Rouge since the terroir is actually more than that but
Pataille Montrecul (Dijon) is really nice as well as the Clos du Roi (Chenove). Bizot also have some nice ones from the northern Cote de Nuits but those are expensive.
The Barthod is amazing but quite a bit more pricey. I think what I like, is to discover growers who have nice terroir but the wines are classified as Bourgogne because of the AOC system. I find Lafarge nice but a bit of a one note wine, unless recent vintages are different. Last one I had is 09.

posts.name: Jeff Brinkman
posts.cooked:
dcornutt:

Can you drink better Bourgogne rouge than Lafarge and Chevillon?
I do like Bachelet but these two just really hit the sweet spot for Bourgogne.

What is your favorite(s)?

BTW, 2012 Lafarge tonight. Refreshing. Red fruited. Not long but very satisfying.

Used to buy a lot of Mugneret-Gibourg’s bourgogne rouge, was always my favorite. For that matter, I always found their Vosne Village to be one of the better Village wines in the Nuits.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked:
Although the Barthod and some of the others mentioned are good, buying a BR at $50 plus defeats
The purpose for me. I do like Meo and Arnoux.

Personally I prefer Bourgogne in riper vintages like 09. The wines, lacking the stuffing of village and 1ers, often strike me as thin and weedy in leaner years. But I also love Sonoma Coast low octane pinots, so not a strictly old world palate.

Agree Lafarge is a standout. M-G and Bachelet too. Never had a Chevillon, need to try.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
Some of my faves from the producers I represent:
-Mugneret Gibourg (a few vosne vineyards)
-Hudelot Noellat (many nuggets of Chambolle)
-Patrice Rion Bons Batons (single parcel chambolle)
-Alain Michelot (two northern nsg parcels) This is a crowd favorite.
-Denis Bachelet (gevrey)
-Michel Lafarge (volnay parcel)
-Robert Groffier (one or two parcels in gevrey?) - This is outrageously good in '13, like all of Nico’s '13s. If you can find some somewhere, get it. I’m sold out.
-Francois Bertheau - (single parcel chambolle) Drinking the ‘13 tonight, first time since tasting it in tank. It is absolutely delightful. Shows the hyper classic face of the vintage, quite bright, pale and pretty, with sneaky power. Many substantial Bourgognes serve as great intros to the house style, and this one shows the hallmark delicacy of Bertheau at his best.
-Michel Noellat (vosne)
-Robert Arnoux (vosne)
-Gerard Julien Cotes de Nuits (comblanchien) This kid (Etienne) is making waves, in a big way. On 10+ Michelin 3 star lists throughout France. While not technically “Bourgogne” for the price point we’re talkin’, a must see. As he said to me once, “La mayonnaise arrive”.
-Thomas Bouley Hautes Cotes de Beaune (above Volnay)
I also adore Ghislaine’s and Cecile’s Bourgognes.
Sooo much value and everyday useful refreshment in those wines.

posts.name: Paul H Galli
posts.cooked:
Big fan of Roty Bourgogne “Pressonier” (given time to mellow out)…

TTT

posts.name: Loren Sonkin
posts.cooked: Do any of you age these wines? If so, how long?

posts.name: Travis Scott
posts.cooked:
Chevillon, for sure. Lafarge, yes, but I’d say less consistently. Maume, same thing, inconsistent but some vintages excellent. Clavelier “Champs d’Argent,” though I haven’t seen it around for a few years. Rouget, also haven’t seen for years, but used to be quite good, and a fun “terroir” experience as 100% Couchey, which I’m not sure exists anywhere else, or at least not from a domaine at this level. Montille and Burguet going back, but nothing all that compelling since the 1999s. A&P “Digoine” if we move out of the Côte d’Or.

Some other stuff that doesn’t really count, as Nick points out, like Roumier, Dugat-Py “Halinard,” Bizot “Chapitre.” In fact, I doubt Rouget would count anymore, either, now that I think about it. Even Bachelet is now beyond the pale.


Loren Sonkin:

Do any of you age these wines? If so, how long?

Yes, same as any other red burgundy.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
My two favorites have long been the Bons Batons of Patrice et Michele Rion and the bourgogne of Mugneret Gibourg (from vineyards that were formerly, ie, before the AOC eliminated the vineyards accross the former RN 74 from Vosne vlllages status, Vosne villages). They have a Chambolle and Vosne aspect, not surprisingly. The villages from Chevillon has a nice Nuits aspect, though is a relatively simpler wine than the rest of their stable.

Aging? As long as a villages wine…or even 1er cru. The upside might be a little more limited. And, in my home, they are the first to get sacrificed to see how a vintage is doing after, say 12-15 years. But, they are likely no “readier” than the rest of the vintage…just less costly to sacrifice.

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked:
Pat Burton:

I picked up some Hudelot Noellat but haven’t tasted one yet. Do you have thoughts Don?

I really like H-N’s Bourgogne rouge. The '09 was excellent. Went through a case and should have bought five at the PC price ($10? $12 maybe)

Roty’s Pressonier is good.

Leroy can be good but variable and now pricey.

Anyone had Fourrier’s BR? I have some on pre-arrival.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked: Mongeard Mugneret makes a good one too. They don’t lather it with all the oak the upper crus get.

posts.name: Paul Jaouen
posts.cooked: I haven’t seen Lamarche mentioned. I really like Nicole’s.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
Loren Sonkin:

Do any of you age these wines? If so, how long?

As others have said, I age them as long as the village wines. I’m enjoying 93 and 95 Bourgognes currently. Personally, unless it’s year like 2009, I don’t particularly like drinking them early, unless it’s soon after release. (there are some that are vinified for early drinking, but I don’t seek these out. most estates just vinify them like their village wines.)

I’d love to see a treatise on Bourgognes, and where they come from with the better estates, and which are declassified higher level wines, etc.).

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Last week, I had two 2005 Bourgognes - one from Truchot and the other from Leroy - that were fabulous, as good as any I have had (esp. the Truchot). They were also still too young!!! We also had a very nice Bourgogne from Taupenot-Merme.

I really like Tremblay’s Bourgogne. And, for a real bargain, I like Jouan’s Passetoutgrains.

Tomorrow night, I will be trying a Hudelot-Noellat Bourgogne - never had that before, will be interesting.

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
+1 for Hudelot-Noellat. Great Bourgogne Rouge every year.

+1 for Michel Noellat. I wasnt’t crazy about their Village wines, but the Bourgogne is excellent.

Denis Mortet Cuvée de Noble Souche and Sylvain Cathiard are maybe my favorites, but they’re also very expensive.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Does anyone know which of these estates do partial/total carbonic maceration for their Bourgognes?

A lot of places used to …and many estates I visited didn’t even show their Bourgognes, as they bottled them quickly after they were made.

Some of those on this thread used to, I know.

And, unfortunately, Bourgognes at houses where the importers add really hefty markups on the wines, usually have such hefty markups on their Bourgognes, too.

Leroy’s Bourgogne has been “pricey” for as long as I’ve been paying attention.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
I could be mistaken, but I can’t think of any of the above producers who do carbonic for Bourgogne. It is treated as “seriously” as the other parcels, perhaps aged slightly differently ie tank or neutral wood most often. I will ask more pointedly in November.
And lest I forget to mention another great bourgogne rouge:
Simon Bize les Perrieres rouge

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked: Good to hear, though that wasn’t always the case with Bourgogne on all on this thread. That I know. A couple used at least partial carbonic a la beaujolais. I used to ask places if I could taste their Bourgognes…and only a few places would let me usually. Some explained it was “different”. So, I delved into it at some point…and let it drop, too. At many places I’ve visited 5 or more times over the years, I never tasted their Bourgogne…and not for lack of trying or interest.

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked: That may just say something about the trend that was wrt to carbonic Stuart. Now many Beaujolais domains are producing wines in a more traditionally burgundian style of elevage. I don’t know how many of them have completely given up carbonic, but I taste many crus that are quite traditional and do not seem to be carbonic.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
I hadn’t known that, Michael. Thanks. Nice to hear, too.

I assume, maybe wrongly, that most gamay in Bourgogne Passetousgrains (PTG) goes through carbonic maceration?

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

I hadn’t known that, Michael. Thanks. Nice to hear, too.

I assume, maybe wrongly, that most gamay in Bourgogne Passetousgrains (PTG) goes through carbonic maceration?

I don’t know the answer to that, and don’t drink much PTG. Just Lafarge sometimes.

I’d honestly be curious to see whether I can detect the carbonic in the same wine, or in a lineup of cru Beaujolais. Sometimes it seems obvious, sometimes I am unsure.

Howard: agree that the '05 from Truchot was also brilliant…Too good. I drank them all.

Mugneret-Gibourg has also been good; needs time from mag.

My Lafarge and Bachelet were not anywhere near ready the last time I tried them.


-mark

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
Count me as a big fan of Lafarge. We also like the Mongeard-Mugneret.
We have enjoyed Lamarche but have not found it very easily.

Last week opened that little '99 from Dom de la Tour, offered by EFW. It drank quite well for a 16 year old 20 buck wine.

posts.name: Ɠřәǥǥ Ɠřәәŋþąųӎ
posts.cooked: Another vote for Lafarge and Groffier, though prices have crept up considerably. Same with Bachelet, which I bought up through 2005 (CT says I paid $15-$18 from '99 through '02). I still have some 02 which I think will still drink well. I also like Cathiard’s rendition and of course Leroy (the only thing I can afford from them). At about $25, I think the '99 Leroy Bourgogne that was around several years ago was a sensational bottle with nice age. In the white department, Roulot is the clear winner.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Howard Cooper:

Last week, I had two 2005 Bourgognes - one from Truchot and the other from Leroy - that were fabulous, as good as any I have had (esp. the Truchot). They were also still too young!!! We also had a very nice Bourgogne from Taupenot-Merme.

I really like Tremblay’s Bourgogne. And, for a real bargain, I like Jouan’s Passetoutgrains.

Tomorrow night, I will be trying a Hudelot-Noellat Bourgogne - never had that before, will be interesting.

The Truchot Bourgognes were killers! Wished I still had some of my 2005s.

Thanks for all the input here. Some wines to track down in this thread.

posts.name: Steven Lawrence
posts.cooked:
Howard Cooper:

Last week, I had two 2005 Bourgognes - one from Truchot and the other from Leroy - that were fabulous, as good as any I have had (esp. the Truchot). They were also still too young!!!

Indeed. We’ve been sacrificing a bottle a year of Truchot’s '05s since 2009, just now starting to open up! His '04s were stellar too. Way transcended the appellation and the vintage.

David Clark’s '07 Pelson was singing recently (with some air), as was Meo-Camuzet’s '10 negotiant Offering.

Cheers

(Hope all is well with you, Howard)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
G. Greenbaum:

In the white department, Roulot is the clear winner.

I think Arnaud Ente also deserves a mention in this class…

The best Bourgogne rouge I’ve had has consistently been Mugneret Gibourg.

The smart but unhelpful answer is Leroy’s 2004.

Does anyone have any experience with recent releases of Emmanuel Rouget’s Bourgogne Passetoutgrains? Just picked up a few of the 2012, but I haven’t tasted.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: In white Leflaive, Roulot, Sauzet, Jean-Marc Vincent and Jean-Claude Boisset are our go to’s. The last two we import.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: a warning to those who purchase bourgogne blanc from roulot: a long time purveyor of roulot informed me that recent vintages of the bourgogne blanc have quite the high percentage of tca on account of a switch to agglomerated cork closure. either way this is not my favorite bottlings. the coche bourgogne is my favorite depending on vintage and is usually in the same ball park price wise. arnaud ente is one of my favorite producers but i have only tried the aligote. for some reason i rarely drink bourgogne rouge as i would rather have a top cuvee of beaujolais for the same price.

posts.name: Scott E. Wade
posts.cooked:
Mugneret-Gibourg in red. You get the polished house style. 2009 and 2010 were terrific. As with most of their offerings, they drink above their level.

In white, Leflaive. This offering always impresses me and this also drinks well above it’s level. Again, I also think this is a smart way to try Leflaive because you get the rich house style here too.

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked: I don’t know about others, but it’s not helpful to me when something “drinks above its level” when it’s also priced above its level. For $50-60 a bottle, it sure ought to. Generally once you get to that range, I’d rather buy less-pedigreed 1ers.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: A cheaper white Bourgogne that is quite good is Dublere. A good value in reds is Domaine des Moirots.
